thursday: The militia's one-eyed founder, Mullah Omar, had promised to restore order to a country shattered by civil war that erupted after the Soviet Union's departure.

puzzles.context_card: When these fighters rolled into Kabul, they came not just with Kalashnikovs but with a blueprint for society that hadn't been seen in centuries. Former president Najibullah was dragged from his UN sanctuary and hanged from a traffic light — his body a message to anyone who doubted the new order. Women who had been doctors and teachers were ordered home, and a generation of girls lost their education overnight.

puzzles.event_formal: Arrest of Augusto Pinochet in London
puzzles.headline: A former dictator recovering from back surgery is handcuffed in a London hospital — and international law changes forever.
puzzles.description: Chilean general Augusto Pinochet, who had ruled through torture and forced disappearances for nearly two decades, was arrested at a private London clinic at the request of a Spanish judge seeking his extradition. His detention triggered a landmark legal battle over whether former heads of state could be prosecuted for crimes against humanity committed in another country. Though ultimately returned to Chile on health grounds, the case permanently shattered the assumption that powerful rulers were untouchable.

thursday: Spanish magistrate Baltasar Garzón issued the arrest warrant, citing the murder and forced disappearance of Spanish citizens during the general's seventeen-year dictatorship.

puzzles.context_card: For decades, Augusto Pinochet had enjoyed the protection of power — dining with world leaders, his past buried in classified files and diplomatic courtesy. Then a Spanish judge had an idea that changed international law: that some crimes are too grave to hide behind national sovereignty. Pinochet spent 503 days under house arrest in a Surrey mansion before being sent home — never tried, but permanently stripped of the invincibility he had counted on.

puzzles.event: Twin Embassy Bombings Strike East Africa
puzzles.event_formal: 1998 United States Embassy Bombings
puzzles.headline: Twin truck bombs detonate minutes apart at American embassies on opposite shores of a continent.
puzzles.description: Massive truck bombs exploded almost simultaneously at US embassies in Nairobi, Kenya and Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, killing more than 220 people and wounding thousands more. The coordinated attacks were later attributed to al-Qaeda, bringing Osama bin Laden to global attention for the first time. The US responded with cruise missile strikes on suspected training camps in Afghanistan and a pharmaceutical factory in Sudan.

friday: The coordinated strikes in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam announced al-Qaeda to the world for the first time, drawing a US retaliation of cruise missiles aimed at Afghanistan and Sudan.

puzzles.context_card: Most people in the West had never heard the name 'al-Qaeda' when two truck bombs went off minutes apart at American embassies in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam. The vast majority of the 224 killed were Kenyan and Tanzanian civilians — not Americans — a detail often lost in how the event is remembered. The bombings were Osama bin Laden's formal introduction to the world, and the consequences played out across the decade that followed.

puzzles.event: Helicopters Lift Last Americans from Saigon
puzzles.event_formal: Fall of Saigon
puzzles.headline: Helicopters lift desperate evacuees from a rooftop while tanks roll into the capital below.
puzzles.description: As North Vietnamese tanks rolled into the South Vietnamese capital, American officials and thousands of Vietnamese allies scrambled to evacuate by helicopter in one of the most chaotic military withdrawals in history. The iconic image of a helicopter hovering above a Saigon apartment building became an enduring symbol of American defeat. The reunification of Vietnam under communist rule followed, ending three decades of war.

friday: Ambassador Graham Martin refused to authorize the evacuation until the Tan Son Nhat air base was already under rocket fire, turning the final twelve hours into a desperate scramble across rooftops.

puzzles.context_card: The photograph of a helicopter hovering above a Saigon rooftop became one of the defining images of the twentieth century — a superpower's retreat compressed into a single frame. The last Americans departed having promised South Vietnam they would never abandon it; about 130,000 Vietnamese managed to flee and millions more were left behind. The country they had known ceased to exist by nightfall.

puzzles.event_formal: East Timorese Independence Referendum
puzzles.headline: A tiny half-island nation votes overwhelmingly to break free — and is immediately set ablaze.
puzzles.description: Nearly 80 percent of East Timorese voters chose independence from Indonesia in a UN-organized referendum, a margin that stunned international observers and the Indonesian government alike. Indonesian-backed militias responded by burning much of the capital Dili and killing hundreds before international peacekeepers could deploy. The vote set in motion the territory's emergence as the world's newest nation three years later.

friday: Indonesian-backed militias burned the capital Dili within hours of the referendum result, forcing Australia to lead an emergency peacekeeping force — the largest it had deployed since Vietnam.

puzzles.context_card: For twenty-four years, East Timor had been occupied by Indonesia in one of the least-reported conflicts of the late Cold War era, with an estimated 100,000 to 180,000 people killed. When the ballot boxes opened, nearly 80 percent had chosen independence — and within hours, militia groups backed by the Indonesian military began burning the country they refused to let go. It took an Australian-led peacekeeping force to stop the destruction and allow a new nation to eventually be born.

puzzles.event_formal: 2002 Venezuelan Coup d'état Attempt
puzzles.headline: A socialist president is declared gone, the White House quietly approves — then he walks back through the palace doors.
puzzles.description: Venezuelan president Hugo Chávez was briefly ousted in a military coup and replaced by a business leader, only to return to power within 48 hours after mass street demonstrations and military defections reversed the takeover. The episode exposed deep political fractures in Venezuela and raised serious questions about US foreknowledge of the plot. It became a defining chapter in Chávez's political mythology and in Latin America's broader story of left-wing resilience.

saturday: A borrowed throne lasted forty-seven hours before the crowds filling the streets of Caracas made their answer known.

puzzles.context_card: For forty-seven hours, a leftist populist was gone — replaced by a businessman in a palace that didn't quite know what to do with the power it had seized. Then the crowds appeared: hundreds of thousands filling the streets of Caracas, demanding the return of the man they had elected. The soldiers reconsidered, and Chávez walked back in as if to prove that power sometimes depends less on guns than on who the people are willing to march for.

puzzles.event: Darfur Genocide Erupts in Western Sudan
puzzles.event_formal: Darfur Genocide
puzzles.headline: Desert villages burn while the world debates whether to call it what it is.
puzzles.description: Armed Arab militias known as the Janjaweed, backed by the Sudanese government, launched a campaign of systematic mass killing and displacement against non-Arab ethnic groups in the Darfur region of western Sudan. Over the following years, hundreds of thousands were killed and more than two million people displaced in what the United States government officially labeled genocide. The crisis became a defining test of the international community's 'responsibility to protect' doctrine — and a test it largely failed.

saturday: Satellite images arrived to document absence, not event — pale ash where villages had stood, the desert having swallowed them before any camera could follow the camel-mounted militias home.

puzzles.context_card: The word 'Darfur' means 'land of the Fur people' — a region that would lend its name to a genocide most of the world struggled to name while it was still happening. Government-backed Arab militias burned villages and poisoned wells, displacing millions into the desert while the UN debated terminology. The United States eventually called it genocide; the killing continued anyway, making it a textbook lesson in the distance between 'never again' as a slogan and 'never again' as a policy.

puzzles.event_formal: Treaty of Maastricht
puzzles.headline: A small Dutch city gives its name to the document that finally answered: what, exactly, is Europe?
puzzles.description: Twelve European nations signed the Maastricht Treaty, formally transforming the European Community into the European Union and establishing a framework for a common currency, European citizenship, and deeper political integration. The agreement built on decades of post-war economic cooperation that had begun as a coal and steel pact among six nations. It set in motion the creation of the euro and defined the shape of European governance for a generation.

saturday: A small Dutch city on a bend in the Maas River gave its name to the document that asked whether Europe was merely a market — or something more.

puzzles.context_card: Before this treaty, 'Europe' was mostly an economic project — a trade zone with shared agricultural subsidies, not a political identity. The agreement signed in a quiet Dutch city created European citizenship, pointed twelve nations toward a common currency, and set the continent on a path that would eventually encompass twenty-seven members. Whether that ambition could hold together would be tested, again and again, in the decades that followed.

puzzles.event: SpaceX Falcon Heavy Makes First Flight
puzzles.event_formal: SpaceX Falcon Heavy Maiden Flight
puzzles.headline: A cherry-red sports car drifts through the cosmos with a space-suited mannequin at the wheel.
puzzles.description: When the world's most powerful operational rocket lifted off from Cape Canaveral, its payload was unlike anything a space agency had ever approved. The two side boosters returned to earth in a synchronized landing that left watching crowds speechless. The car and its passenger, Starman, are still orbiting the sun somewhere between Earth and Mars.
puzzles.context_card: Elon Musk chose the payload himself — his own Tesla Roadster, with a mannequin in a spacesuit and David Bowie playing on the stereo. The synchronized side-booster landing was an engineering showstopper, but the image of a car floating against the curvature of Earth became the defining frame of the era. It's still out there, drifting on a solar orbit nobody planned particularly carefully.

puzzles.event: Japan's Earthquake and Tsunami Strike the Northeast
puzzles.event_formal: 2011 Tōhoku Earthquake and Tsunami
puzzles.headline: A magnitude 9.0 offshore earthquake sends walls of water swallowing entire towns in minutes.
puzzles.description: The most powerful earthquake ever recorded in Japan struck offshore, unleashing a tsunami that overwhelmed seawalls and turned coastal cities into debris fields within minutes. The disaster triggered a nuclear crisis at Fukushima Daiichi that became the worst since Chernobyl. Nearly 20,000 people perished, and the quake was so powerful it shifted Japan's main island by 2.4 metres.
puzzles.context_card: Footage of fishing boats and buildings swept through city streets like toys remains among the most haunting images of the century. Entire communities — Rikuzentakata, Minamisanriku — simply ceased to exist. Seismologists noted the quake released enough energy to shorten Earth's day by 1.8 microseconds and permanently altered the region's coastline. The cleanup and displacement lasted years.

puzzles.event: World Cup Debuts on African Soil
puzzles.event_formal: 2010 FIFA World Cup, South Africa
puzzles.headline: The sound of vuvuzelas fills stadiums as a continent finally hosts football's biggest tournament.
puzzles.description: South Africa became the first African nation to host a FIFA World Cup, a milestone Nelson Mandela had championed for decades. Spain claimed their first-ever world title in a tense final played in Johannesburg. The tournament's defining sound — the relentless drone of plastic horns — divided opinion worldwide and dominated broadcast coverage as much as the football itself.
puzzles.context_card: The vuvuzela nearly broke the tournament's broadcast engineers. Spanish television lobbied FIFA to ban them outright. But in Johannesburg, the noise felt like the roar of a continent finally given its moment on the world stage. South Africa's own team went out in the group stage, yet the stadiums stayed packed — the symbolism of hosting mattered more than the scorelines.

puzzles.event: Egypt's President Falls After Mass Protests
puzzles.event_formal: Egyptian Revolution — Resignation of Hosni Mubarak
puzzles.headline: After thirty years in power, an Egyptian president is swept from office by eighteen days of mass protest.
puzzles.description: Millions occupied Tahrir Square in Cairo demanding an end to Hosni Mubarak's three-decade rule, inspired by a revolution that had just toppled another long-serving leader nearby. The military stepped aside as protests swelled, and a vice president delivered a brief televised announcement that stunned the world. The Arab Spring had claimed its most significant figure.

monday: Millions camped in a square named for the Arabic word for 'liberation,' defying three decades of emergency law and a government-imposed curfew that proved impossible to enforce
The fall came via a single terse sentence read on state television by a vice president, ending the rule of a former air force commander who had survived four documented assassination attempts

puzzles.context_card: Tahrir Square became the symbol of a generation — young Egyptians live-streaming their revolution on smartphones, something the outgoing president never fully grasped. The military's quiet decision to step aside was the decisive moment almost nobody saw coming in the tense final hours. What followed was complicated and often dark, but the moment the announcement came through felt, briefly, like history bending under the weight of a crowd.

puzzles.event: NASA Confirms Liquid Water Flows on Mars
puzzles.event_formal: NASA Announcement of Recurring Slope Lineae on Mars
puzzles.headline: Dark seasonal streaks creeping down Martian slopes turn out to be something scientists had long dared to hope for.
puzzles.description: Data from the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter revealed that mysterious dark lines descending sun-facing Martian slopes were consistent with briny liquid water, a finding that transformed the search for extraterrestrial life. The discovery suggested Mars might still host conditions capable of supporting microbial life beneath its surface. The announcement was delivered with NASA's characteristic mix of understatement and barely-contained excitement.

monday: Orbiting spectrometers detected hydrated salt compounds in seasonal dark smears that appeared and faded on sun-facing slopes — chemistry that pointed unmistakably to a briny, intermittent flow

The recurring streaks, designated RSL by planetary scientists, had resisted explanation for years before a spectroscopic analysis finally revealed what was leaving those dark traces on ancient crater walls

puzzles.context_card: The press conference opened with 'Mars is not the dry, arid planet that we thought of in the past.' The briny water — probably perchlorate-rich and too frigid and salty to drink — wouldn't fill a bathtub. But its seasonal reappearance on a geologically active world changed the calculus of where to look for life in the solar system. Mars, it turns out, is still wet. Just barely.

puzzles.event: China Lands First Probe on Moon's Far Side
puzzles.event_formal: Chang'e 4 Lunar Mission — Von Kármán Crater Landing
puzzles.headline: A spacecraft touches down on the Moon's most enigmatic hemisphere — a surface no probe has ever reached from below.
puzzles.description: China's Chang'e 4 became the first spacecraft in history to successfully soft-land on the Moon's far side, the perpetually Earth-hidden hemisphere that had never been explored up close. Because the far side cannot communicate directly with Earth, the mission required a relay satellite positioned at a gravitational equilibrium point beyond the Moon. The rover Yutu-2 then began exploring the ancient Von Kármán impact crater.

monday: A relay satellite parked at a stable gravitational equilibrium point beyond the Moon was the critical prerequisite, solving a communications problem no previous lunar mission had ever needed to address
The rover's landing site was a crater so geologically ancient that its floor may contain material excavated from the Moon's original deep mantle by an enormous primordial impact

puzzles.context_card: The Moon's far side is not 'the dark side' — it receives just as much sunlight as the near side. It's simply tidally locked away from our view forever. Humans had photographed it from orbit since the Soviet Luna 3 in 1959, but landing there meant you couldn't talk to your spacecraft directly. China built a relay satellite named Queqiao — the Magpie Bridge — and solved the problem. The landing happened on New Year's Day.

puzzles.event: Gunmen Attack Charlie Hebdo's Paris Newsroom
puzzles.event_formal: Charlie Hebdo Shooting, Paris
puzzles.headline: Masked gunmen storm a satirical magazine's Paris offices, killing cartoonists and journalists in broad daylight.
puzzles.description: Armed attackers killed twelve people in a morning assault on the offices of Charlie Hebdo, a French satirical weekly known for its provocative cartoons. The attack was followed by a kosher supermarket siege that killed four more, triggering France's largest anti-terrorism mobilisation in decades. Millions poured into French streets in the days that followed, and solidarity rallies were held in cities across the world.

tuesday: The attack on a satirical French weekly with a history of printing provocative cartoons was followed by days of nationwide manhunts and a second hostage crisis at a kosher supermarket on the outskirts of the capital
The editor who survived a previous firebombing of the same offices was killed alongside seven colleagues during the Wednesday morning assault, which lasted less than ten minutes

puzzles.context_card: The phrase 'Je suis Charlie' spread faster than any political slogan in recent memory, appearing on banners from Lagos to Tokyo within hours. The march of world leaders through Paris two days later was the most high-profile solidarity event in years. Charlie Hebdo had survived a firebombing four years earlier; after this attack, it published its next issue on schedule with a print run of eight million — normally it sold 30,000 copies.

puzzles.event: Christchurch Earthquake Devastates New Zealand City
puzzles.event_formal: 2011 Christchurch Earthquake
puzzles.headline: A lunchtime tremor collapses a cathedral and buries workers in office towers within seconds.
puzzles.description: A magnitude 6.3 earthquake struck just outside New Zealand's second-largest city when it was packed with midday crowds, killing 185 people and injuring thousands more. The historic Christchurch Cathedral lost its spire, and the central business district became a sealed exclusion zone for years. It remains the deadliest natural disaster in New Zealand in over eight decades.

tuesday: New Zealand's second-largest city lost its Victorian Gothic cathedral spire and had its historic downtown sealed off for years after a midday tremor brought towers down without warning
The disaster struck at lunchtime when offices were fully occupied, and the collapse of a single language school building housing international students accounted for more than a hundred of the deaths

puzzles.context_card: The February quake was technically an aftershock — a larger earthquake had hit the same region five months earlier, causing mostly property damage and no deaths. But this one struck at 12:51 pm when the streets and buildings were full. The CTV Building, a language school packed with international students, pancake-collapsed in seconds, killing 115 people. Christchurch has been rebuilding, and passionately debating how to rebuild, ever since.

puzzles.event: Colombia Signs Historic FARC Peace Agreement
puzzles.event_formal: Colombian Peace Agreement with FARC-EP
puzzles.headline: A half-century of jungle warfare in South America's most biodiverse nation reaches a negotiating table.
puzzles.description: The Colombian government and the FARC guerrilla organization signed a peace deal ending more than fifty years of armed conflict that had killed over 220,000 people and displaced millions more. In an extraordinary twist, Colombian voters narrowly rejected the initial agreement in a national referendum by the slimmest of margins. A revised deal was ratified by congress weeks later without a second public vote.

tuesday: The guerrilla group had operated from jungle strongholds since the early Cold War era, outlasting a dozen governments and every military campaign launched against it over five decades
Four years of secret negotiations in Havana produced a landmark agreement that was then struck down by voters in a referendum decided by fewer than 54,000 votes out of thirteen million cast

puzzles.context_card: The talks took four years in Havana, with Cuba and Norway as unlikely co-guarantors. When the referendum failed by 0.4 percent — one of the tightest national votes in recent history — the deal looked dead. The negotiators went back, made revisions, and passed the agreement through congress. The FARC's founding generation were long dead by then; some of its fighters had never known a Colombia not at war with itself.

puzzles.event_formal: Publication of Ulysses by James Joyce
puzzles.headline: A single June day in Dublin rewrites what a novel can be
puzzles.description: When Sylvia Beach of Shakespeare and Company agreed to print it in Paris, she was doing what every mainstream publisher had refused. The novel had already been serialized in an American literary magazine, only to be seized as obscene before it could finish. Its release drew a clear before-and-after line across the history of prose fiction.

wednesday: Banned in the United States as obscene before it ever appeared between hard covers
First published by a small American-run Paris bookshop called Shakespeare and Company

puzzles.context_card: James Joyce modeled his novel on Homer's Odyssey, mapping eighteen episodes onto a single day in Dublin — June 16th, still celebrated worldwide as Bloomsday. Sylvia Beach's tiny Left Bank shop took the risk that no commercial publisher would, printing the first edition on the author's birthday. The book permanently expanded what prose could do with time, consciousness, and the texture of a single mind.

puzzles.event_formal: World Premiere of George Gershwin's Rhapsody in Blue
puzzles.headline: A clarinet's upward wail announced jazz had arrived at the concert hall
puzzles.description: George Gershwin composed the piece in roughly five weeks, reportedly hearing its architecture fully formed in his head on a train ride to Boston. Performed at Aeolian Hall in New York by Paul Whiteman's orchestra, it brought jazz idioms into formal concert life for the first time at that scale. In the audience sat Rachmaninoff, Stravinsky, and Jascha Heifetz.

wednesday: Composed in about five weeks after a newspaper announcement pressured the composer to deliver something for an orchestral jazz concert
Premiered in Manhattan in a concert billed by bandleader Paul Whiteman as 'An Experiment in Modern Music'

puzzles.context_card: Gershwin had no fully written piano part at the premiere — he improvised his solo from a sketched lead sheet while the orchestra played from the printed score. The iconic opening clarinet glissando began as a joke by clarinetist Ross Gorman, a crude parody of a scale that Gershwin loved and insisted on keeping. It remains one of the most performed American orchestral works ever written.

puzzles.event_formal: Hiram Bingham III's Rediscovery of Machu Picchu
puzzles.headline: A Yale professor hacks through cloud-forest vines to find a forgotten city
puzzles.description: Guided by a local farmer's young son, American explorer Hiram Bingham III climbed through dense jungle above the Urubamba River to find an Inca citadel standing in near-perfect condition. The site had never truly been lost to local Peruvians, but Bingham's photographs for National Geographic brought it to global attention. His expedition shipped thousands of artifacts to Yale — the opening act of a diplomatic dispute that lasted a century.

wednesday: The fifteenth-century Inca citadel sat above a river gorge in the Peruvian Andes, invisible from below and unknown to the outside world
A Yale University expedition brought the site to international attention through a landmark National Geographic photo spread

puzzles.context_card: Machu Picchu was likely built as a royal estate for the Inca emperor Pachacuti and abandoned within a century as the Spanish conquest unraveled the empire. The guide who led Bingham to the ruins was a small local boy — the site was never hidden from Andean communities who farmed its terraces into the twentieth century. Peru and Yale did not settle their dispute over Bingham's exported artifacts until a century later, when the university finally returned the collection.
